section.title: Woe to Ephraim and Jerusalem
section.sectionRationale: This opens the sequence of woes, chapters 28 to 33, that confront false trust, first in Ephraim's drunken pride, then in Jerusalem's scoffing leaders who have made a covenant with death. At its center stands one of Scripture's most quoted verses, the tested cornerstone laid in Zion, later applied directly to Christ in Romans 9 and 1 Peter 2.
section.thesis: The LORD who will trample Ephraim's fading crown of pride also confronts Jerusalem's scoffing rulers, who have made a covenant with death itself, promising instead a tested and precious cornerstone in Zion on which the one who believes will never be shaken.
section.frameNote: Four movements: woe to Ephraim's drunken, fading crown; the stumbling priests and prophets taught only line on line; the scoffers' covenant with death answered by the sure cornerstone; and the farmer's parable showing that even ordinary wisdom comes from the LORD.

verses.text: Woe to the majestic crown of Ephraim's drunkards, to the fading flower of his glorious splendor, set on the summit above the fertile valley, the pride of those overcome by wine.
Behold, the Lord has one who is strong and mighty. Like a hailstorm or destructive tempest, like a driving rain or flooding downpour, he will smash that crown to the ground.
The majestic crown of Ephraim's drunkards will be trampled underfoot.
The fading flower of his beautiful splendor, set on the summit above the fertile valley, will be like a ripe fig before the summer harvest: Whoever sees it will take it in his hand and swallow it.
On that day the LORD of Hosts will be a crown of glory, a diadem of splendor to the remnant of His people,
a spirit of justice to him who sits in judgment, and a strength to those who repel the onslaught at the gate.

units.label: Stumbling priests and prophets
units.note: line on line, precept on precept

verses.text: These also stagger from wine and stumble from strong drink: Priests and prophets reel from strong drink and are befuddled by wine. They stumble because of strong drink, muddled in their visions and stumbling in their judgments.
For all their tables are covered with vomit; there is not a place without filth.
Whom is He trying to teach? To whom is He explaining His message? To infants just weaned from milk? To babies removed from the breast?
For they hear: “Order on order, order on order, line on line, line on line; a little here, a little there.”
Indeed, with mocking lips and foreign tongues, He will speak to this people
to whom He has said: “This is the place of rest, let the weary rest; this is the place of repose.” But they would not listen.

Then the word of the LORD to them will become: “Order on order, order on order, line on line, line on line; a little here, a little there,” so that they will go stumbling backward and will be injured, ensnared, and captured.

units.label: The scoffers' covenant with death, and the sure cornerstone

verses.text: Therefore hear the word of the LORD, O scoffers who rule this people in Jerusalem.

For you said, “We have made a {{beritMavet|covenant with death}}; we have fashioned an agreement with Sheol. When the overwhelming scourge passes through it will not touch us, because we have made lies our refuge and falsehood our hiding place.”
So this is what the Lord GOD says: “See, I lay a stone in Zion, a {{ebenBochan|tested stone}}, a precious cornerstone, a sure foundation; the one who believes will never be shaken.
I will make justice the measuring line and righteousness the level. Hail will sweep away your refuge of lies, and water will flood your hiding place.
Your covenant with death will be dissolved, and your agreement with Sheol will not stand. When the overwhelming scourge passes through, you will be trampled by it.
As often as it passes through, it will carry you away; it will sweep through morning after morning, by day and by night.” The understanding of this message will bring sheer terror.
Indeed, the bed is too short to stretch out on, and the blanket too small to wrap around you.
For the LORD will rise up as at Mount Perazim. He will rouse Himself as in the Valley of Gibeon, to do His work, His strange work, and to perform His task, His disturbing task.
So now, do not mock, or your shackles will become heavier. Indeed, I have heard from the Lord GOD of Hosts a decree of destruction against the whole land.

units.label: The farmer's parable of God's wisdom
units.note: ordinary skill comes from the LORD

verses.text: Listen and hear my voice. Pay attention and hear what I say.
Does the plowman plow for planting every day? Does he continuously loosen and harrow the soil?
When he has leveled its surface, does he not sow caraway and scatter cumin? He plants wheat in rows and barley in plots, and rye within its border.
For his God instructs and teaches him properly.
Surely caraway is not threshed with a sledge, and the wheel of a cart is not rolled over the cumin. But caraway is beaten out with a stick, and cumin with a rod.
Grain for bread must be ground, but it is not endlessly threshed. Though the wheels of the cart roll over it, the horses do not crush it.
This also comes from the LORD of Hosts, who is wonderful in counsel and excellent in wisdom.

beritMavet.gloss: A covenant with death, the scoffing rulers' boast that they had secured immunity from disaster through political scheming and deceit. The LORD answers this false covenant not with a rebuke alone but with an alternative, a real and sure foundation stone in Zion.

ebenBochan.gloss: A tested or tried stone, examined and proven reliable before being used as a cornerstone, the foundation piece that determines the whole structure's alignment. The New Testament applies this precise image to Christ, the stone the builders rejected who became the cornerstone, Romans 9:33, 1 Peter 2:6.

basic.context: This chapter opens Isaiah's sequence of woes with a taunt against Ephraim's drunken pride, her once-splendid crown soon to be trampled, before turning to Jerusalem's own scoffing leaders, whose spiritual drunkenness leaves them mocking the prophet's simple, repetitive teaching. At its heart the LORD exposes their trust in a boastful covenant with death and answers it with a far better foundation: a tested, precious cornerstone laid in Zion.
basic.hermeneutics: Ephraim's judgment reflects real, imminent Assyrian conquest of the northern kingdom, while the woe against Jerusalem's leaders addresses the same generation's arrogant political scheming, likely trust in Egyptian alliance, described as a covenant with death. The cornerstone promise, verse 16, functions in its own context as a promise of a secure foundation for those who trust the LORD rather than political maneuvering, while the New Testament reveals its fuller, messianic meaning in Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: Jerusalem's rulers boast of a berit mavet, a covenant with death, trusting lies and falsehood as their refuge. The LORD's answer employs even bochan, a tested stone, a term for a stone examined and proven reliable, the exact image later applied directly to Christ as the cornerstone in Romans 9:33 and 1 Peter 2:6.
basic.typology: The tested cornerstone laid in Zion, on which the one who believes will never be shaken, is one of the Old Testament's clearest messianic images, directly identified with Christ in the New Testament as the stone the builders rejected that became the very cornerstone, quoted repeatedly of Him, Matthew 21:42, Acts 4:11, Romans 9:33, 1 Peter 2:6.
basic.christ: Isaiah's cornerstone, tested, precious, and sure, on which the one who believes will never be shaken, is quoted directly of Christ in Romans 9:33 and 1 Peter 2:6, and Jesus applies the related stone imagery to Himself in Matthew 21:42. Where Jerusalem's leaders trusted a false covenant with death, Christ is the true and living foundation stone who alone secures those who believe in Him against the overwhelming scourge of final judgment.
basic.god: God exposes both drunken national pride and cynical political scheming as forms of the same underlying refusal to trust Him; He is patient enough to teach His people line upon line, yet just enough to let persistent mockery become their own trap. Even His judgment, His strange and disturbing work, serves His ultimate purpose of establishing a sure foundation for those who will believe.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 21:42-44 applies the rejected stone that became the cornerstone directly to Jesus. Romans 9:33 quotes Isaiah 28:16 to explain Israel's stumbling over Christ. 1 Peter 2:6-8 develops the cornerstone image fully, contrasting believers who trust the stone with those who stumble over it. 2 Samuel 5:20 and Joshua 10:10 record the historical battles at Perazim and Gibeon that verse 21 recalls.
basic.oneStory: This chapter exposes the futility of both drunken pride and cynical political trust, a covenant with death that cannot actually shelter anyone from judgment, and answers both with the promise of a sure and tested cornerstone in Zion. That cornerstone is Christ, the one foundation on which everyone who believes will never be shaken, however fiercely the storms of judgment sweep through.

context.literary: Structured as four scenes moving from a taunting woe against Ephraim's fading pride, through an increasingly mocking exchange over the prophet's simple teaching method, to the chapter's theological center, the scoffers' false covenant answered by the true cornerstone, and closing with an agricultural parable illustrating that even ordinary human wisdom is a gift from God.
context.historical: Likely addresses the years leading up to Assyria's conquest of the northern kingdom, Ephraim, in 722 BC, and the subsequent temptation for Judah's leaders to seek security through alliance with Egypt rather than trust in the LORD, the same political scheming Isaiah elsewhere calls a covenant with death, echoing the crisis addressed later in chapters 30 and 31.
context.canonical: Contains one of the most frequently quoted Old Testament verses in the New Testament, the cornerstone of verse 16, cited or echoed in Matthew 21, Romans 9, Ephesians 2:20, and 1 Peter 2, making this chapter foundational to the New Testament's understanding of Christ as the rejected yet vindicated cornerstone of God's true temple.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confront both Ephraim's drunken pride and Jerusalem's cynical political scheming as failures to trust the LORD, and to promise that a sure and tested foundation stone, not human alliances or lies, is the only true security against coming judgment.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter describes specific historical failures, drunken pride and false political security, and promises a specific messianic foundation; it does not prescribe Isaiah's exact teaching method, line on line, as a universal pedagogical formula. Its abiding truth is that trust in anything but the LORD's own provided foundation ultimately fails.

debates.question: What does order on order, line on line, a little here, a little there, verses 10 and 13, actually describe: mocking imitation of Isaiah's simple teaching, or a genuine description of how God patiently teaches?

views.label: The scoffers' mocking imitation of Isaiah's simple style
views.case: The immediate context, drunken priests and prophets unable to handle solid teaching, suggests these are the scoffers' own words, mimicking and mocking Isaiah's supposedly simplistic, repetitive instruction as fit only for infants.

views.label: A genuine description of God's patient, incremental teaching method
views.case: The phrase's repetition in verse 13, describing how God's own word will come to them, suggests it may describe a real, patient pedagogical pattern that the scoffers rejected, turning what could have been gentle instruction into a trap because of their refusal to listen.

debates.bearing: Either reading agrees that the scoffers rejected simple, patient instruction from the LORD, and that their rejection, whether beginning as mockery of Isaiah's style or resistance to God's actual teaching method, turned the very words meant to instruct them into their downfall.

study.originalLanguages: Jerusalem's rulers boast of a berit mavet, a covenant with death, believing lies can shelter them from disaster. The LORD answers with even bochan, a tested and proven stone, a foundation image the New Testament repeatedly and directly identifies with Christ, the cornerstone on whom whoever believes will never be shaken or put to shame.

typology.type: The tested, precious cornerstone laid in Zion
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the stone the builders rejected who became the cornerstone, quoted of Him in Matthew 21:42, Romans 9:33, and 1 Peter 2:6-8.

study.christ: The cornerstone promise of verse 16 is one of the most directly quoted Old Testament texts about Christ in the New Testament: Paul cites it in Romans 9:33 to explain why Israel stumbled over her own Messiah, and Peter develops it fully in 1 Peter 2:6-8, contrasting those who trust this stone with those who stumble over it. Jesus Himself applies the related image of the rejected stone become the cornerstone to His own coming rejection and vindication, Matthew 21:42. Where Jerusalem's leaders trusted a covenant with death that could not save them, Christ is the true and tested foundation, sure enough that everyone who believes in Him will never be shaken.
study.god: God exposes both drunken national pride and cynical political scheming as the same underlying refusal to trust Him, and He is willing to let persistent mockery of His patient instruction become the very trap that catches the mockers. Yet even in pronouncing this strange and disturbing work of judgment, His ultimate aim is to establish, for everyone who will believe, an utterly secure and tested foundation stone in Zion.

crossReferences.note: Jesus applies the rejected stone that became the cornerstone directly to Himself and His coming rejection.
Paul quotes Isaiah 28:16 to explain why Israel stumbled over Christ as a stone of stumbling.
Develops the cornerstone image fully, contrasting believers who trust the stone with those who reject it and stumble.
Records the LORD's victory at Baal-perazim, the historical event verse 21 recalls as a picture of God's decisive, even disruptive, action.

study.oneStory: This chapter exposes the futility of drunken pride and cynical political trust, a covenant with death that cannot shelter anyone from judgment, and answers both with the promise of a sure, tested cornerstone in Zion. That cornerstone is fulfilled in Christ, the one foundation on which everyone who believes will never be shaken, whatever storms of judgment sweep through the land.

quiz.q: What image describes Ephraim's coming downfall in verses 1 to 4?

options.text: A majestic but fading crown of pride, trampled underfoot like a ripe fig swallowed at first sight.
options.note: Verses 1 to 4 use exactly this image of a fading, easily consumed crown.

options.text: A fortress that successfully withstands every attack.
options.note: The opposite; the image is one of collapse and trampling, not successful defense.

options.text: A river overflowing its banks in blessing.
options.note: Not the image used here; the imagery is of a fading crown and trampled pride.

options.text: A vineyard that produces an unusually abundant harvest.
options.note: This vineyard image appears in a different Isaiah passage, not this specific oracle against Ephraim's pride.

quiz.q: Why does the LORD teach His people order on order, line on line, a little here, a little there?

options.text: Because the priests and prophets were spiritually stupefied by drink and could not handle more.
options.note: Verses 7 to 10 describe their drunken, muddled state as the context for this simple teaching style.

options.text: Because Israel's children were unusually gifted and needed advanced instruction.
options.note: The opposite; the simple style is connected to spiritual stupor, not advanced giftedness.

options.text: Because Isaiah had run out of things to say.
options.note: Not the reason given; the text ties this style to the audience's drunken incapacity.

options.text: Because foreign nations had banned complex teaching.
options.note: Not stated in the text; the explanation given relates to Israel's own spiritual stupor from wine.

quiz.q: What have Jerusalem's scoffing rulers boasted of making, according to verse 15?

options.text: A covenant with death and an agreement with Sheol.
options.note: Verse 15 states this boast explicitly and directly.

options.text: A covenant of peace with all the surrounding nations.
options.note: The opposite; their boast is specifically about escaping death and disaster through deceit, not general peace.

options.text: A new alliance with the priesthood.
options.note: Not mentioned in this verse; the boast concerns a covenant with death, not the priesthood.

options.text: A treaty to rebuild the temple.
options.note: Unrelated to this verse's actual content about a false covenant with death.

quiz.q: What does the LORD promise instead of the scoffers' covenant with death, in verse 16?

options.text: A tested, precious cornerstone laid in Zion, on which the one who believes will never be shaken.
options.note: Verse 16 gives this exact promise, later quoted of Christ in the New Testament.

options.text: A new set of political alliances with Egypt.
options.note: The opposite of the point; the LORD offers a divine foundation, not another human alliance.

options.text: An immediate end to all future judgment.
options.note: The chapter continues to describe coming judgment even after this promise; the cornerstone is a secure foundation, not a cancellation of judgment altogether.

options.text: A promise that Jerusalem's walls will never be breached.
options.note: The promise given is specifically about a tested cornerstone and belief, not a guarantee about the city's walls.

quiz.q: What point does the farmer's parable in verses 23 to 29 make?

options.text: Even a farmer's practical wisdom in planting and threshing comes from God's own instruction.
options.note: Verses 26 and 29 state directly that this ordinary skill and wisdom comes from the LORD.

options.text: Farming is a purely human skill unrelated to God.
options.note: The opposite; the parable explicitly credits God as the source of the farmer's wisdom.

options.text: God punishes farmers who use different methods for different crops.
options.note: The parable actually commends this variation as wise, not something to be punished.

options.text: Farming should be avoided in favor of trusting God alone.
options.note: The parable does not counsel avoiding farming; it shows that even skillful practical work is itself a gift and instruction from God.

questions: Ephraim's crown of pride, though splendid, was fading and soon swallowed like a ripe fig. What source of pride in your own life might be more fragile than it currently feels?
The scoffers trusted a covenant with death, lies as their refuge. What lies or false securities might you be quietly resting on instead of trusting God's actual promises?
The cornerstone promise is that the one who believes will never be shaken. Where do you need that steady confidence right now, in a season that otherwise feels shaky?

The priests and prophets were too spiritually dulled by indulgence to receive more than simple, repeated instruction. Are there habits or indulgences in your own life that might be dulling your capacity to hear God clearly?
The farmer's ordinary wisdom in planting and threshing is credited to God's own instruction. Where in your daily work or skills might you be overlooking God's hand and provision?

translationNotes.note: Never be shaken can also be rendered will not act hastily or will not be ashamed, reflecting a range in how the Hebrew idiom is understood; the Septuagint's rendering, will not be put to shame, underlies Paul's quotation in Romans 9:33 and 10:11.

Mount Perazim and the Valley of Gibeon recall specific earlier battles, 2 Samuel 5:20 and Joshua 10:10, where the LORD fought for Israel against her enemies; here the same LORD rises to act, strangely, against His own people.

Order on order, line on line likely renders short, repetitive Hebrew syllables that may have mimicked either simplistic baby talk or a genuine teaching cadence, contributing to the debated tone of this phrase.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 28:1-29. Poetic line breaks joined into running verse text; no em dashes were present in the source text for this chapter.
